name: Nautilus
description: A hybrid blockbuilding and algorithmic method for the 3x3. Nautilus builds a 1x2x3 block (similar to Roux's first block), then solves the remaining pieces using a combination of last slot and last layer techniques. It blends the intuitive blockbuilding of Roux with algorithmic efficiency, offering a unique solving experience that emphasizes both creativity and speed.

steps.name: First Block (FB)
steps.description: Build a 1x2x3 block on the left side of the cube, typically on the D-layer. This block includes the DL edge, and two corner-edge pairs forming a 1x2x3 shape. This step is identical to Roux first block building.
tips: Plan the entire first block during inspection
Use wide moves and rotations to build efficiently
Study multiple solutions for the same scramble to improve flexibility

steps.name: Second Square
steps.description: Build a 1x2x2 square (a 'square' consisting of one corner-edge pair plus the DR edge) on the right side of the bottom layer. This partially completes the second block without fully extending it to a full 1x2x3.
tips: Look ahead during first block to plan this step
Keep the first block preserved while building

steps.name: Last Slot + Last Layer (LSLL)
steps.description: Solve the last F2L slot (the remaining corner-edge pair on the right side) and the last layer in fewer steps than traditional approaches. Various sub-methods can be used here including ZBLS + ZBLL, Winter Variation + PLL, or other LSLL approaches. The key idea is to influence the last layer state while inserting the last pair.
tips: Learn VLS or Winter Variation to orient last layer edges during last slot insertion
ZBLL knowledge significantly reduces total move count
Practice recognizing last layer state before inserting the last pair

pros: Lower move count than CFOP due to efficient blockbuilding
Flexible and creative first two steps
LSLL approach can skip OLL entirely with enough algorithm knowledge
cons: LSLL sub-methods require many algorithms (especially ZBLL)
Fewer resources and community support compared to mainstream methods
creator: Trangium
